content:
# Judges Chapter 2 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), c. 1008 CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/JDG_page_268.jpg, JDG_page_269.jpg

1. Now the angel of the LORD went up from Gilgal to Bochim. And he said, "I brought you up from Egypt and brought you into the land that I swore to give to your fathers. I said, 'I will never break my covenant with you,

2. and you shall make no covenant with the inhabitants of this land; you shall break down their altars.' But you have not obeyed my voice. What is this you have done?

3. So now I say, I will not drive them out before you, but they shall become thorns in your sides, and their gods shall be a snare to you."

4. As soon as the angel of the LORD spoke these words to all the people of Israel, the people lifted up their voices and wept.

5. And they called the name of that place Bochim. And they sacrificed there to the LORD.

6. When Joshua dismissed the people, the people of Israel went each to his inheritance to take possession of the land.

7. And the people served the LORD all the days of Joshua, and all the days of the elders who outlived Joshua, who had seen all the great work that the LORD had done for Israel.

8. And Joshua the son of Nun, the servant of the LORD, died at the age of one hundred and ten years.

9. And they buried him within the boundaries of his inheritance in Timnath-heres, in the hill country of Ephraim, north of the mountain of Gaash.

10. And all that generation also were gathered to their fathers. And there arose another generation after them who did not know the LORD or the work that he had done for Israel.

11. And the people of Israel did what was evil in the sight of the LORD and served the Baals.

12. And they abandoned the LORD, the God of their fathers, who had brought them out of the land of Egypt. They went after other gods, from among the gods of the peoples who were around them, and bowed down to them. And they provoked the LORD to anger.

13. They abandoned the LORD and served Baal and the Ashtaroth.

14. So the anger of the LORD was kindled against Israel, and he gave them over to plunderers, who plundered them. And he sold them into the hand of their surrounding enemies, so that they could no longer withstand their enemies.

15. Whenever they marched out, the hand of the LORD was against them for harm, as the LORD had warned and as the LORD had sworn to them. And they were in terrible distress.

16. Then the LORD raised up judges, who saved them out of the hand of those who plundered them.

17. Yet they did not listen to their judges, for they whored after other gods and bowed down to them. They soon turned aside from the way in which their fathers had walked, who had obeyed the commandments of the LORD, and they did not do so.

18. Whenever the LORD raised up judges for them, the LORD was with the judge, and he saved them from the hand of their enemies all the days of the judge. For the LORD was moved to pity by their groaning because of those who afflicted and oppressed them.

19. But whenever the judge died, they turned back and were more corrupt than their fathers, going after other gods, serving them and bowing down to them. They did not drop any of their practices or their stubborn ways.

20. So the anger of the LORD was kindled against Israel, and he said, "Because this people have transgressed my covenant that I commanded their fathers and have not obeyed my voice,

21. I will no longer drive out before them any of the nations that Joshua left when he died,

22. in order to test Israel by them, whether they will take care to walk in the way of the LORD as their fathers did, or not."

23. So the LORD left those nations, not driving them out quickly, and he did not give them into the hand of Joshua.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Chapter 2 is the theological prologue to the entire book. It moves from the angel's indictment at Bochim (vv.1-5) through a retrospective on Joshua's generation (vv.6-10) to the programmatic cycle statement (vv.11-19) and the divine declaration of testing (vv.20-23). The key insight: the cycle is not repetition but deterioration — "more corrupt than their fathers" (v.19).

**Verse 18 — "Moved to pity by their groaning":** A remarkable statement. The text does not say Israel repented (shub) or confessed (yadah). It says they groaned (na'aq), and God was moved to pity (nacham). God responds to suffering, not just to contrition. This is one of the most compassionate theological statements in the Hebrew Bible.

---
## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### v.3 — "thorns in your sides" vs. "a constant temptation to you"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "thorns in your sides" | l'tziddim — "for sides/flanks" |
| LOGOS | "thorns in your sides" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "They will be thorns in your sides, and their gods will be a constant temptation to you" | Adds "constant" to "temptation"; "temptation" for moqesh (snare) |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Two issues: (1) moqesh means "snare/trap" — a physical hunting device — not "temptation" (an internal psychological state). The metaphor is external entrapment, not internal desire. (2) PNEUMA adds "constant," which is not in the text. The snare is dangerous precisely because it is not always visible — "constant temptation" implies continuous awareness, while a "snare" implies hidden danger.

**Status:** Minor. Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Snare ≠ temptation.

---

### v.14 — "he sold them into the hand of their enemies" vs. "he turned them over to their enemies"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he sold them into the hand of their enemies" | vayyimk'rem b'yad oy'veihem |
| LOGOS | "he sold them into the hand of their surrounding enemies" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "He turned them over to their enemies all around" | "Turned them over" for "sold" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Hebrew verb מכר (makhar) means "to sell" — a commercial metaphor. God "sold" Israel to their enemies, as property is sold to a buyer. PNEUMA's "turned them over" loses the economic metaphor. The selling language appears throughout Judges (3:8, 4:2, 10:7) and creates a bitter irony: Israel, who was redeemed (bought back) from Egypt, is now sold to new masters because of their unfaithfulness.

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. The "selling" metaphor connects to the redemption/purchase theology of the Exodus.

---

### v.17 — "whored after other gods" vs. "prostituted themselves by worshiping other gods"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "they whored after other gods" | zanah acharei elohim acherim |
| LOGOS | "they whored after other gods" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "prostituted themselves by worshiping other gods" | Adds "by worshiping"; passive "prostituted themselves" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Hebrew zanah acharei means "to whore after" — active, not passive. PNEUMA's "prostituted themselves by worshiping" adds an explanatory gloss ("by worshiping") and makes the action reflexive-passive rather than active. The author uses the raw verb deliberately: Israel actively pursued other gods, as a person actively seeks illicit partners. The sexual metaphor is meant to shock.

**Status:** Minor. Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Active "whored after" vs. passive "prostituted themselves."

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA changes metaphor type | 1 | "Temptation" for "snare" (v.3) |
| PNEUMA loses economic metaphor | 1 | "Turned over" for "sold" (v.14) |
| PNEUMA softens sexual metaphor | 1 | "Prostituted themselves" for "whored after" (v.17) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 2's disagreements center on metaphor. The Hebrew Bible uses vivid, often disturbing metaphors — selling, whoring, snaring — and PNEUMA consistently softens or abstracts them. The most significant is v.14: the "selling" metaphor connects directly to the Exodus redemption theology. If God "bought" Israel from Egypt (redemption = purchase), then "selling" them back to enemies reverses the Exodus. PNEUMA's "turned over" loses this economic-theological connection.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
